answer:
**San Hyan Tattvajnana, Chapter 1.**

The assignments of patron divinities (*deva*) to three classes of blades (*asi*) by social function appear in the context of describing *buddhi-rajas* (intellect dominated by passion), where the *Rākṣasa*, *Daitya*, and *Dānava* are linked to blades used by kings, farmers, and priests, respectively.[^1] This immediately precedes further elaboration on *buddhi-tamas*, but the text soon frames the distinction among these blade classes within a blacksmith simile to illustrate how tools (tongs, hammer, anvil, file) and raw iron submit entirely to the smith's will, while only the finished blade enacts good and evil.[^2]

The three blade-divinity assignments are quoted verbatim as follows:

> Now, the reality of this is that the *Rākṣasa* is the patron divinity of the blades that are the weapons of a king. All kinds of blades that are currently used to take life have the *Rākṣasa* as their deity.[^1]

> The *Daitya* is the patron divinity of the blades that are the implements of farmers, such as a *luke* or a *pṛkul* (types of agricultural blades). All kinds of blades used as a means to produce a livelihood have the *Daitya* as their deity.[^1]

> The *Dānava* is the patron divinity of the blades that are the implements of a priest, such as a *pamiso*, *kartrī*, or *karayu* (types of ritual knives). The *Dānava* is their deity.[^1]

[^1]: *San Hyan Tattvajnana*, [paragraph](/works/unknown-san-hyan-tattvajnana-1f29bd/01/translation/#p-cbb351).
[^2]: *San Hyan Tattvajnana*, [paragraph](/works/unknown-san-hyan-tattvajnana-1f29bd/01/translation/#p-9c664a).
